subject: Chemistry
text: Biomolecules: These are the macromolecules essential for survival of life, e.g. carbohydrotes, proteins, etc.
text_html: Biomolecules: These are the macromolecules essential for survival of life, e.g. carbohydrotes, proteins, etc.

subject: Chemistry
text: Carbohydrates: These are polyhydroxy aldehydes or ketones or their derivatives, e.g. glucose.
text_html: Carbohydrates: These are polyhydroxy aldehydes or ketones or their derivatives, e.g. glucose.

subject: Chemistry
text: Preparation: From sucrose: {tex}C_{12}H_{22}O_{11}+H_2O\xrightarrow{H^+}\underset{Glu\cos e}{C_6H_{12}O_6}+\underset{Fructose}{C_6H_{12}O_6}{/tex} (only from sucrose) From starch: (C 6 H 10 O 5 ) n + nH 2 O {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} C 12 H 22 O 11 + H 2 O {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\underset{glucos e}{2C_6H_{12}O_6}{/tex}
text_html:
Preparation:

  1. From sucrose: {tex}C_{12}H_{22}O_{11}+H_2O\xrightarrow{H^+}\underset{Glu\cos e}{C_6H_{12}O_6}+\underset{Fructose}{C_6H_{12}O_6}{/tex} (only from sucrose)
  2. From starch: (C6H10O5)n + nH2O {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} C12H22O11 + H2O {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\underset{glucos e}{2C_6H_{12}O_6}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
text: Carbohydrates: Glucose It is obtained by hydrolysing starch or cane sugar. its reactions are as: Glucose {tex}\frac{\mathrm{HI}}{\Delta}{/tex} n-hexane:
text_html:
Carbohydrates: Glucose

  • It is obtained by hydrolysing starch or cane sugar.
  • its reactions are as:

Glucose {tex}\frac{\mathrm{HI}}{\Delta}{/tex}n-hexane:

subject: Chemistry
text: Carbohydrates: Sucrose It contains D(+) glucose and D(-) fructose joined together by {tex}\beta{/tex} -1, 2-glycosidic linkage. It is also called invert sugar.
text_html:
Carbohydrates: Sucrose

  • It contains D(+) glucose and D(-) fructose joined together by {tex}\beta{/tex}-1, 2-glycosidic linkage.
  • It is also called invert sugar.

subject: Chemistry
text: Starch: It contains two components: amylose (water soluble, 20%) and amylopectin (80%, water insoluble). It is the reserve food of plants.
text_html:
Starch:

  • It contains two components: amylose (water soluble, 20%) and amylopectin (80%, water insoluble).
  • It is the reserve food of plants.

subject: Chemistry
text: Cellulose: Most abundant in plants and contains glucose units, joined together by {tex}\beta{/tex} -1, 4-glycosidic linkage.
text_html: Cellulose: Most abundant in plants and contains glucose units, joined together by {tex}\beta{/tex}-1, 4-glycosidic linkage.

subject: Chemistry
text: Carbohydrates: These are optically active polyhydroxy aldehydes or ketones or the compounds which produce these on hydrolysis.
text_html: Carbohydrates: These are optically active polyhydroxy aldehydes or ketones or the compounds which produce these on hydrolysis.

subject: Chemistry
text: Classification : Monosaccharides: Those carbohydrates which cannot be hydrolysed into further simpler carbohydrates. e.g, glucose, fructose, galactose etc. Disaccharides: Those carbohydrates which produces two monosaccharides on hydrolysis. e.g, sucrose, maltose and lactose. Oligosaccharides: Those carbohydrates which give two to ten monosaccharides on hydrolysis. Polysaccharides: Those carbohydrates which on hydrolysis give large number of monosaccharides on hydrolysis. e.g, starch, cellulose, glycogen.
text_html:
Classification :

  1. Monosaccharides: Those carbohydrates which cannot be hydrolysed into further simpler carbohydrates. e.g, glucose, fructose, galactose etc.
  2. Disaccharides: Those carbohydrates which produces two monosaccharides on hydrolysis. e.g, sucrose, maltose and lactose.
  3. Oligosaccharides: Those carbohydrates which give two to ten monosaccharides on hydrolysis.
  4. Polysaccharides: Those carbohydrates which on hydrolysis give large number of monosaccharides on hydrolysis. e.g, starch, cellulose, glycogen.

subject: Chemistry
text: Sugar: Carbohydrates which are sweet in taste. Reducing sugars: Those which reduce Fehling's or Tollens' eagent due to availability of free aldehydic groups. e.g, glucose, fructose, galactose. Non-reducing sugars: Those which do not reduce Fehling's or Tollens' reagent. They do not have free aldehydic group. e.g, sucrose.
text_html:
Sugar: Carbohydrates which are sweet in taste.

  1. Reducing sugars: Those which reduce Fehling's or Tollens' eagent due to availability of free aldehydic groups. e.g, glucose, fructose, galactose.
  2. Non-reducing sugars: Those which do not reduce Fehling's or Tollens' reagent. They do not have free aldehydic group. e.g, sucrose.

subject: Chemistry
text: Fischer structure: (+) glucose has 'D' configuration as shown: 'D'- means - OH group on first chiral 'C' from the bottom is on right hand and (+) means it is dextrorotatory i.e., it rotates plane polarized light towards right.
text_html:
Fischer structure:
(+) glucose has 'D' configuration as shown:

'D'- means - OH group on first chiral 'C' from the bottom is on right hand and (+) means it is dextrorotatory i.e., it rotates plane polarized light towards right.

subject: Chemistry
text: Reactions of glucose:
text_html: Reactions of glucose:

subject: Chemistry
text: Objections against open chain structure of glucose: The open chain structure was unable to explain the following reactions: It does not give the 2,4-DNP test, Schiff's test and does not form the hydrogensulphite addition product with NaHSO 3 The pentacetate of glucose does not react with NH 2 OH, indicating the absence of free aldehydic group. Glucose exist in 2 different crystalline forms {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex} forms. These are called anomers. They differ in optical rotation, they also differ in melting point. After which a close chain (cyclic) structure of glucose was proposed by Haworth. * Anomers are isomers which have a different configuration at C-1 functional group
text_html:
Objections against open chain structure of glucose:
The open chain structure was unable to explain the following reactions:

  1. It does not give the 2,4-DNP test, Schiff's test and does not form the hydrogensulphite addition product with NaHSO3
  2. The pentacetate of glucose does not react with NH2OH, indicating the absence of free aldehydic group.
  3. Glucose exist in 2 different crystalline forms {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex} forms. These are called anomers. They differ in optical rotation, they also differ in melting point.

After which a close chain (cyclic) structure of glucose was proposed by Haworth.
* Anomers are isomers which have a different configuration at C-1 functional group

subject: Chemistry
text: Glycosidic linkage: The linkage between two monosaccharide units through oxygen is called the glycosidic linkage.
text_html: Glycosidic linkage: The linkage between two monosaccharide units through oxygen is called the glycosidic linkage.

subject: Chemistry
text: Introduction to Carbohydrates Carbohydrates are organic compounds produced mainly by plants. Common examples include cane sugar, glucose, and starch. They were initially considered as hydrates of carbon due to their formula $$ C_x(H_2O)_y $$.
text_html: Introduction to Carbohydrates Carbohydrates are organic compounds produced mainly by plants. Common examples include cane sugar, glucose, and starch. They were initially considered as hydrates of carbon due to their formula $$ C_x(H_2O)_y $$.

subject: Chemistry
text: Formula and Classification Although many compounds fit the general formula, they may not all be classified as carbohydrates. For example, acetic acid fits the formula but isn't a carbohydrate. Carbohydrates are defined chemically as optically active polyhydroxy aldehydes or ketones.
text_html: Formula and Classification Although many compounds fit the general formula, they may not all be classified as carbohydrates. For example, acetic acid fits the formula but isn't a carbohydrate. Carbohydrates are defined chemically as optically active polyhydroxy aldehydes or ketones.

subject: Chemistry
text: Sugars and Saccharides The sweet carbohydrates are referred to as sugars. Sucrose is the common sugar for households, while lactose is found in milk. Carbohydrates are also known as saccharides, deriving from the Greek word for sugar, "sakcharon."
text_html: Sugars and Saccharides The sweet carbohydrates are referred to as sugars. Sucrose is the common sugar for households, while lactose is found in milk. Carbohydrates are also known as saccharides, deriving from the Greek word for sugar, "sakcharon."

subject: Chemistry
text: Monosaccharides -These are carbohydrates that cannot be broken down further into simpler units. -Examples include glucose, fructose, and ribose. -They are categorized based on the functional group: `aldose` (with an aldehyde group) and `ketose` (with a keto group).
text_html: Monosaccharides -These are carbohydrates that cannot be broken down further into simpler units. -Examples include glucose, fructose, and ribose. -They are categorized based on the functional group: `aldose` (with an aldehyde group) and `ketose` (with a keto group).

subject: Chemistry
text: Classification Based on Carbon Atoms The classification of monosaccharides can be determined by the number of carbon atoms: Triose (3 carbons) Tetrose (4 carbons) Pentose (5 carbons) Hexose (6 carbons) Each can also be further classified as either an aldo or keto version.
text_html:
Classification Based on Carbon Atoms The classification of monosaccharides can be determined by the number of carbon atoms:
  • Triose (3 carbons)
  • Tetrose (4 carbons)
  • Pentose (5 carbons)
  • Hexose (6 carbons)
Each can also be further classified as either an aldo or keto version.

subject: Chemistry
text: Disaccharides Disaccharides are carbohydrates that, upon hydrolysis, yield two monosaccharide molecules. The bond connecting these two molecules is known as a glycosidic linkage. Common examples include sucrose, maltose, and lactose.
text_html: Disaccharides Disaccharides are carbohydrates that, upon hydrolysis, yield two monosaccharide molecules. The bond connecting these two molecules is known as a glycosidic linkage. Common examples include sucrose, maltose, and lactose.

subject: Chemistry
text: Different Types of Disaccharides Sucrose: Hydrolyzes into D-(+)-glucose and D-(-)-fructose and is a non-reducing sugar. Maltose: Composed of two α-D-glucose units; considered a reducing sugar. Lactose: Found in milk, composed of β-D-galactose and β-D-glucose.
text_html:
Different Types of Disaccharides
  • Sucrose: Hydrolyzes into D-(+)-glucose and D-(-)-fructose and is a non-reducing sugar.
  • Maltose: Composed of two α-D-glucose units; considered a reducing sugar.
  • Lactose: Found in milk, composed of β-D-galactose and β-D-glucose.

subject: Chemistry
text: Polysaccharides Polysaccharides are carbohydrates yielding many monosaccharides upon hydrolysis. They mainly function as food storage or structural materials. Starch: Main plant storage polysaccharide, consisting of amylose and amylopectin. Cellulose: Found in plants, composed of β-D-glucose units. Glycogen: Storage carbohydrate in animals, known as animal starch.
text_html:
Polysaccharides Polysaccharides are carbohydrates yielding many monosaccharides upon hydrolysis. They mainly function as food storage or structural materials.
  • Starch: Main plant storage polysaccharide, consisting of amylose and amylopectin.
  • Cellulose: Found in plants, composed of β-D-glucose units.
  • Glycogen: Storage carbohydrate in animals, known as animal starch.

subject: Chemistry
text: Glucose -Glucose is a fundamental monosaccharide, found naturally in fruits and honey. -It is a key building block for larger carbohydrates, such as starch and cellulose. -Exists in two forms, α and β, which are based on the hydroxyl group's position.
text_html: Glucose -Glucose is a fundamental monosaccharide, found naturally in fruits and honey. -It is a key building block for larger carbohydrates, such as starch and cellulose. -Exists in two forms, α and β, which are based on the hydroxyl group's position.

subject: Chemistry
text: Fructose -A ketohexose obtained from disaccharide sucrose. -Found in fruits, honey, and vegetables. -Exists in two cyclic forms, both comprising five-membered rings similar to furan.
text_html: Fructose -A ketohexose obtained from disaccharide sucrose. -Found in fruits, honey, and vegetables. -Exists in two cyclic forms, both comprising five-membered rings similar to furan.

subject: Chemistry
text: Amino acids: These contain an amino (-NH 2 ) and an acidic (-COOH) group and are therefore amphoteric in nature. In solution, they exist in the form of twitter ion (a dipolar ion).
text_html: Amino acids: These contain an amino (-NH2) and an acidic (-COOH) group and are therefore amphoteric in nature. In solution, they exist in the form of twitter ion (a dipolar ion).

subject: Chemistry
text: Denaturation of protein: Destruction of the native state of protein is denaturation. It can be brought by physical and chemical methods. The 2 o and 3 o structures are destroyed, only 1 o structure is retained.
text_html: Denaturation of protein: Destruction of the native state of protein is denaturation. It can be brought by physical and chemical methods. The 2o and 3o structures are destroyed, only 1o structure is retained.

subject: Chemistry
text: Proteins: These are polymers of {tex}\alpha{/tex} -amino acids. Zwitter lon: Ion containing positive as well as negative charge is called zwitter ion. e.g. {tex}R-\underset{{}^+{NH}_3}{\underset\vert CH}-\overset O{\overset{\vert\vert}C}-O^-{/tex}
text_html:
Proteins: These are polymers of {tex}\alpha{/tex}-amino acids.
Zwitter lon: Ion containing positive as well as negative charge is called zwitter ion.
e.g. {tex}R-\underset{{}^+{NH}_3}{\underset\vert CH}-\overset O{\overset{\vert\vert}C}-O^-{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
text: Structure of Proteins Primary structure: It shows the sequence of amino acids in a Polypeptide chain. Secondary structure: It is formed due to H bonding and may be {tex}\alpha{/tex} -helix or {tex}\beta{/tex} -pleated sheet structure. Tertiary Structure: Represents overall folding of the polypeptide chain. Quaternary structure: Protein can be composed of two or more polypeptide chains called sub-units. The spatial arrangement of these sub-units with respect to each other is quaternary structure of the protein.
text_html:
Structure of Proteins
Primary structure: It shows the sequence of amino acids in a Polypeptide chain.
Secondary structure: It is formed due to H bonding and may be {tex}\alpha{/tex}-helix or {tex}\beta{/tex}-pleated sheet structure.
Tertiary Structure: Represents overall folding of the polypeptide chain.
Quaternary structure: Protein can be composed of two or more polypeptide chains called sub-units. The spatial arrangement of these sub-units with respect to each other is quaternary structure of the protein.

subject: Chemistry
text: Types of Proteins: Fibrous Protein: Polypeptide chains run parallel or anti-parallel and held together by hydrogen and disulphide bonds. e.g. keratin, collagen Globular Protein: Chains of polypeptide coil around to give a spherical shape. e.g. insulin, albumin
text_html:
Types of Proteins:
Fibrous Protein: Polypeptide chains run parallel or anti-parallel and held together by hydrogen and disulphide bonds. e.g. keratin, collagen
Globular Protein: Chains of polypeptide coil around to give a spherical shape. e.g. insulin, albumin

subject: Chemistry
text: Proteins Tertiary Structure: Represents overall folding of the polypeptide chain.
text_html:
Proteins
Tertiary Structure: Represents overall folding of the polypeptide chain.

subject: Chemistry
text: Proteins: These are macro molecules made up of amino acids joined by amide linkage (-CONH-) is called as peptide linkage. These are required for growth and development of the body.
text_html: Proteins: These are macro molecules made up of amino acids joined by amide linkage (-CONH-) is called as peptide linkage. These are required for growth and development of the body.

subject: Chemistry
text: Native state of protein: The parental state or the natural state in which the protein is found.
text_html: Native state of protein: The parental state or the natural state in which the protein is found.

subject: Chemistry
text: Overview of Proteins Proteins are abundant biomolecules essential for life, found in various foods such as milk, cheese, and meat. They form the structure and facilitate physiological functions. Required for body growth and maintenance.
text_html:
Overview of Proteins
Proteins are abundant biomolecules essential for life, found in various foods such as milk, cheese, and meat.
  • They form the structure and facilitate physiological functions.
  • Required for body growth and maintenance.

subject: Chemistry
text: Amino Acids Basics Amino acids are building blocks of proteins containing –NH2 and –COOH groups. Classified by the position of the amino group: α, β, γ... Only α-amino acids result from protein hydrolysis.
text_html:
Amino Acids Basics
Amino acids are building blocks of proteins containing –NH2 and –COOH groups.
  • Classified by the position of the amino group: α, β, γ...
  • Only α-amino acids result from protein hydrolysis.

subject: Chemistry
text: Naming and Representation of Amino Acids Amino acids have trivial names related to characteristics or origins. Example: Glycine is sweet-tasting. Tyrosine is from cheese. Represented by three-letter and sometimes one-letter symbols.
text_html:
Naming and Representation of Amino Acids
Amino acids have trivial names related to characteristics or origins.
  • Example: Glycine is sweet-tasting. Tyrosine is from cheese.
  • Represented by three-letter and sometimes one-letter symbols.

subject: Chemistry
text: Classification of Amino Acids Categorized as acidic, basic, or neutral based on amino and carboxyl group balance. Nonessential: Synthesized in the body. Essential: Obtained through diet.
text_html:
Classification of Amino Acids
Categorized as acidic, basic, or neutral based on amino and carboxyl group balance.
  • Nonessential: Synthesized in the body.
  • Essential: Obtained through diet.

subject: Chemistry
text: Properties of Amino Acids Amino acids are colorless, crystalline solids with salt-like behavior. Water-soluble with high melting points. Exist as zwitterions exhibiting amphoteric behavior.
text_html:
Properties of Amino Acids
Amino acids are colorless, crystalline solids with salt-like behavior.
  • Water-soluble with high melting points.
  • Exist as zwitterions exhibiting amphoteric behavior.

subject: Chemistry
text: Optical Activity in Amino Acids Except glycine, all α-amino acids are optically active. Most occur naturally in L-form. Represented by -NH2 on the left in structural formulas.
text_html:
Optical Activity in Amino Acids
Except glycine, all α-amino acids are optically active.
  • Most occur naturally in L-form.
  • Represented by -NH2 on the left in structural formulas.

subject: Chemistry
text: Protein Structure Overview Proteins are polymers linked by peptide bonds from α-amino acids. Peptide bond: An amide linkage between –COOH and –NH2 groups. Polypeptides form dipeptides, tripeptides, and more.
text_html:
Protein Structure Overview
Proteins are polymers linked by peptide bonds from α-amino acids.
  • Peptide bond: An amide linkage between –COOH and –NH2 groups.
  • Polypeptides form dipeptides, tripeptides, and more.

subject: Chemistry
text: Types and Shapes of Proteins Proteins have fibrous or globular molecular structures. Fibrous proteins: Insoluble, forming fibers like keratin. Globular proteins: Soluble, spherical such as insulin.
text_html:
Types and Shapes of Proteins
Proteins have fibrous or globular molecular structures.
  • Fibrous proteins: Insoluble, forming fibers like keratin.
  • Globular proteins: Soluble, spherical such as insulin.

subject: Chemistry
text: Levels of Protein Structure Primary: Sequence of amino acids on polypeptides. Secondary: a-helix & b-pleated sheet formations with hydrogen bonds. Tertiary: Overall 3D structure due to folding of polypeptides. Quaternary: Multi-sub-unit spatial arrangement.
text_html:
Levels of Protein Structure
  1. Primary: Sequence of amino acids on polypeptides.
  2. Secondary: a-helix & b-pleated sheet formations with hydrogen bonds.
  3. Tertiary: Overall 3D structure due to folding of polypeptides.
  4. Quaternary: Multi-sub-unit spatial arrangement.

subject: Chemistry
text: Protein Denaturation Proteins lose activity when subjected to physical or chemical changes. Examples: Egg white coagulation, curdling of milk. Preserves primary structure while other structures are disrupted.
text_html:
Protein Denaturation
Proteins lose activity when subjected to physical or chemical changes.
  • Examples: Egg white coagulation, curdling of milk.
  • Preserves primary structure while other structures are disrupted.

subject: Chemistry
text: Enzymes: There are the proteins which catalyse reaction occuring in human body, they are also known as biocatalysts e.g. Invertase, Zymase etc.
text_html: Enzymes: There are the proteins which catalyse reaction occuring in human body, they are also known as biocatalysts e.g. Invertase, Zymase etc.

subject: Chemistry
text: Main characteristics of enzymes: It speed up the biological reaction upto million times. It is highly specific and work on lock and key theory. It is highly sensitive to pH and temperature.
text_html:
Main characteristics of enzymes:

  1. It speed up the biological reaction upto million times.
  2. It is highly specific and work on lock and key theory.
  3. It is highly sensitive to pH and temperature.

subject: Chemistry
text: Concept of Life and Chemical Reactions Life depends on coordination of various chemical reactions. Includes important processes like digestion and energy production.
text_html:
Concept of Life and Chemical Reactions
  • Life depends on coordination of various chemical reactions.
  • Includes important processes like digestion and energy production.

subject: Chemistry
text: Role of Enzymes Biocatalysts such as enzymes facilitate reactions. Operate under mild conditions within the body.
text_html:
Role of Enzymes
  • Biocatalysts such as enzymes facilitate reactions.
  • Operate under mild conditions within the body.

subject: Chemistry
text: Enzyme Characteristics Most enzymes are globular proteins. Specific to reactions and substrates. Named after the substrates they act upon.
text_html:
Enzyme Characteristics
  • Most enzymes are globular proteins.
  • Specific to reactions and substrates.
  • Named after the substrates they act upon.

subject: Chemistry
text: Naming of Enzymes Named after the substrate or the type of reaction they catalyze. Example: Maltase catalyzes the conversion of maltose to glucose.
text_html:
Naming of Enzymes
  • Named after the substrate or the type of reaction they catalyze.
  • Example: Maltase catalyzes the conversion of maltose to glucose.

subject: Chemistry
text: Chemical Reaction Involving Maltase Maltose is converted to two glucose molecules. Reaction specific to the enzyme maltase.
text_html:
Chemical Reaction Involving Maltase
  • Maltose is converted to two glucose molecules.
  • Reaction specific to the enzyme maltase.

subject: Chemistry
text: Oxidoreductase Enzymes Enzymes catalyzing oxidation-reduction reactions. Example: Transfer electrons between molecules.
text_html:
Oxidoreductase Enzymes
  • Enzymes catalyzing oxidation-reduction reactions.
  • Example: Transfer electrons between molecules.

subject: Chemistry
text: Enzyme Nomenclature Names commonly end with "-ase." Reflects the substrate or reaction type.
text_html:
Enzyme Nomenclature
  • Names commonly end with "-ase."
  • Reflects the substrate or reaction type.

subject: Chemistry
text: Mechanism of Enzyme Action Enzymes reduce activation energy needed for reactions. Function similarly to chemical catalysts.
text_html:
Mechanism of Enzyme Action
  • Enzymes reduce activation energy needed for reactions.
  • Function similarly to chemical catalysts.

subject: Chemistry
text: Enzyme Efficiency in Energy Reduction Example: Activation energy for acid hydrolysis of sucrose reduces significantly with sucrase. Facilitates reaction progression.
text_html:
Enzyme Efficiency in Energy Reduction
  • Example: Activation energy for acid hydrolysis of sucrose reduces significantly with sucrase.
  • Facilitates reaction progression.

subject: Chemistry
text: Enzyme Activation Energy Comparison Acid hydrolysis of sucrose: 6.22 kJ mol -1 . Enzyme-mediated hydrolysis by sucrase: 2.15 kJ mol -1 .
text_html:
Enzyme Activation Energy Comparison
  • Acid hydrolysis of sucrose: 6.22 kJ mol-1.
  • Enzyme-mediated hydrolysis by sucrase: 2.15 kJ mol-1.

subject: Chemistry
text: Vitamins: These are essential but in very small amounts. Vitamins A, D, E and K are fat soluble while rest are water soluble.
text_html: Vitamins: These are essential but in very small amounts. Vitamins A, D, E and K are fat soluble while rest are water soluble.

subject: Chemistry
text: Deficiency Diseases: Vitamin Deficiency Diseases A Xerophithalmia B 1 Beri-beri B 12 Pernicious anaemia C Scurvy D Rickets E Infertility K Increased blood clotting time H Loss of hair
text_html:
Deficiency Diseases:

Vitamin Deficiency Diseases
A Xerophithalmia
B1 Beri-beri
B12 Pernicious anaemia
C Scurvy
D Rickets
E Infertility
K Increased blood clotting time
H Loss of hair

subject: Chemistry
text: Definition of Vitamins Organic compounds needed in small quantities for health. Deficiency can lead to diseases. Not synthesized by the body.
text_html:
Definition of Vitamins
  • Organic compounds needed in small quantities for health.
  • Deficiency can lead to diseases.
  • Not synthesized by the body.

subject: Chemistry
text: Synthesis and Sources Plants synthesize most vitamins. Some gut bacteria produce needed vitamins.
text_html:
Synthesis and Sources
  • Plants synthesize most vitamins.
  • Some gut bacteria produce needed vitamins.

subject: Chemistry
text: Vitamin Classification Fat Soluble Vitamins : A, D, E, K are soluble in fats and oils. Stored in liver & adipose tissue. Water Soluble Vitamins : B group and C are water-soluble. Must be regularly supplied since they are not stored (except B 12 ).
text_html:
Vitamin Classification
  1. Fat Soluble Vitamins: A, D, E, K are soluble in fats and oils. Stored in liver & adipose tissue.
  2. Water Soluble Vitamins: B group and C are water-soluble. Must be regularly supplied since they are not stored (except B12).

subject: Chemistry
text: Chemical Structure Complexity Vitamins belong to various chemical classes. Initially thought to have amino groups, hence termed "Vitamine". 'E' was dropped later as most lacked amino groups.
text_html:
Chemical Structure Complexity
  • Vitamins belong to various chemical classes.
  • Initially thought to have amino groups, hence termed "Vitamine".
  • 'E' was dropped later as most lacked amino groups.

subject: Chemistry
text: Vitamin Naming Conventions Designated by alphabets - A, B, C, D, etc. Sub-groups exist - B 1 , B 2 , B 6 , B 12 .
text_html:
Vitamin Naming Conventions
  • Designated by alphabets - A, B, C, D, etc.
  • Sub-groups exist - B1, B2, B6, B12.

subject: Chemistry
text: Vitamin A Sources : Fish liver oil, carrots, butter, milk. Deficiency Disease : Xerophthalmia - hardening of cornea.
text_html:
Vitamin A
  • Sources: Fish liver oil, carrots, butter, milk.
  • Deficiency Disease: Xerophthalmia - hardening of cornea.

subject: Chemistry
text: Vitamin B Complex B 1 (Thiamine) : Prevents Beri beri. B 2 (Riboflavin) : Prevents cheilosis & digestive issues. B 6 (Pyridoxine) : Prevents convulsions. B 12 : Prevents pernicious anemia.
text_html:
Vitamin B Complex
  • B1 (Thiamine): Prevents Beri beri.
  • B2 (Riboflavin): Prevents cheilosis & digestive issues.
  • B6 (Pyridoxine): Prevents convulsions.
  • B12: Prevents pernicious anemia.

subject: Chemistry
text: Vitamin C Sources : Citrus fruits, amla, green leafy vegetables. Deficiency Disease : Scurvy - bleeding gums.
text_html:
Vitamin C
  • Sources: Citrus fruits, amla, green leafy vegetables.
  • Deficiency Disease: Scurvy - bleeding gums.

subject: Chemistry
text: Vitamin D Sources : Sunlight exposure, fish, egg yolk. Deficiency Disease : Rickets & osteomalacia - bone deformities.
text_html:
Vitamin D
  • Sources: Sunlight exposure, fish, egg yolk.
  • Deficiency Disease: Rickets & osteomalacia - bone deformities.

subject: Chemistry
text: Vitamin E & K Vitamin E : Found in vegetable oils, prevents fragile RBCs. Vitamin K : Found in green leafy vegetables, essential for blood clotting.
text_html:
Vitamin E & K
  • Vitamin E: Found in vegetable oils, prevents fragile RBCs.
  • Vitamin K: Found in green leafy vegetables, essential for blood clotting.

subject: Chemistry
text: DNA RNA Double helical Single stranded Sugar is 2-deoxyribose Sugar is ribose Base: A, T, G, C Bases: A, U, G, C Property of replication Do not replicate It is responsible for transmission of heredity Character Helps in protein biosynthesis
text_html:
DNA RNA
Double helical Single stranded
Sugar is 2-deoxyribose Sugar is ribose
Base: A, T, G, C Bases: A, U, G, C
Property of replication Do not replicate
It is responsible for transmission of heredity Character Helps in protein biosynthesis

subject: Chemistry
text: Nucleic acids: These are biomolecules which are long chain polymers of nucleotides. They are of two types: Deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) Ribonucleic acid (RNA)
text_html:
Nucleic acids: These are biomolecules which are long chain polymers of nucleotides. They are of two types:

  1. Deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA)
  2. Ribonucleic acid (RNA)

subject: Chemistry
text: Nucleus and Heredity The nucleus of a living cell is responsible for the transmission of inherent characters, known as heredity.
text_html:
Nucleus and Heredity

The nucleus of a living cell is responsible for the transmission of inherent characters, known as heredity.

subject: Chemistry
text: Chromosomes and Heredity Chromosomes, located in the cell nucleus, are responsible for heredity. They comprise proteins and nucleic acids.
text_html:
Chromosomes and Heredity
  • Chromosomes, located in the cell nucleus, are responsible for heredity.
  • They comprise proteins and nucleic acids.

subject: Chemistry
text: Types of Nucleic Acids There are two main types: DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) and RNA (ribonucleic acid). Nucleic acids are called polynucleotides as they are long chain polymers of nucleotides.
text_html:
Types of Nucleic Acids
  • There are two main types: DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) and RNA (ribonucleic acid).
  • Nucleic acids are called polynucleotides as they are long chain polymers of nucleotides.

subject: Chemistry
text: Chemical Composition of Nucleic Acids Complete hydrolysis yields a pentose sugar, phosphoric acid, and nitrogenous bases. In DNA, the sugar is β-D-2-deoxyribose; in RNA, it is β-D-ribose.
text_html:
Chemical Composition of Nucleic Acids
  • Complete hydrolysis yields a pentose sugar, phosphoric acid, and nitrogenous bases.
  • In DNA, the sugar is β-D-2-deoxyribose; in RNA, it is β-D-ribose.

subject: Chemistry
text: Bases in Nucleic Acids DNA contains four bases: adenine (A), guanine (G), cytosine (C), and thymine (T). RNA contains adenine, guanine, cytosine, and uracil (U).
text_html:
Bases in Nucleic Acids
  1. DNA contains four bases: adenine (A), guanine (G), cytosine (C), and thymine (T).
  2. RNA contains adenine, guanine, cytosine, and uracil (U).

subject: Chemistry
text: Structure of Nucleic Acids A nucleoside forms when a base attaches to the sugar's 1' position. A nucleotide forms when a nucleoside links to phosphoric acid at the 5' sugar position.
text_html:
Structure of Nucleic Acids
  • A nucleoside forms when a base attaches to the sugar's 1' position.
  • A nucleotide forms when a nucleoside links to phosphoric acid at the 5' sugar position.

subject: Chemistry
text: Nucleotide Linkages Nucleotides are connected by phosphodiester linkage between 5' and 3' carbon atoms of the pentose sugar.
text_html:
Nucleotide Linkages

Nucleotides are connected by phosphodiester linkage between 5' and 3' carbon atoms of the pentose sugar.

subject: Chemistry
text: Primary and Secondary Structures Primary structure: sequence of nucleotides in the nucleic acid chain. Secondary structure: DNA has a double helix; RNA can have folds.
text_html:
Primary and Secondary Structures
  • Primary structure: sequence of nucleotides in the nucleic acid chain.
  • Secondary structure: DNA has a double helix; RNA can have folds.

subject: Chemistry
text: Watson-Crick DNA Structure Double helix structure proposed by Watson and Crick. Two complementary strands are held together by hydrogen bonds. Adenine pairs with thymine, cytosine pairs with guanine.
text_html:
Watson-Crick DNA Structure
  • Double helix structure proposed by Watson and Crick.
  • Two complementary strands are held together by hydrogen bonds.
  • Adenine pairs with thymine, cytosine pairs with guanine.

subject: Chemistry
text: Functions of Nucleic Acids DNA is the chemical basis of heredity, maintaining species identity over time. RNA synthesizes proteins, guided by DNA.
text_html:
Functions of Nucleic Acids
  1. DNA is the chemical basis of heredity, maintaining species identity over time.
  2. RNA synthesizes proteins, guided by DNA.

subject: Chemistry
text: Hormones: These are the chemicals secreted by Endocrine glands.
text_html: Hormones: These are the chemicals secreted by Endocrine glands.

subject: Chemistry
text: Role of Hormones Hormones are intercellular messengers produced by endocrine glands, released directly into the bloodstream, and move to their target sites for action.
text_html:
Role of Hormones
Hormones are intercellular messengers produced by endocrine glands, released directly into the bloodstream, and move to their target sites for action.

subject: Chemistry
text: Chemical Nature of Hormones Hormones have varied chemical structures: Steroids (e.g., estrogens, androgens) Polypeptides (e.g., insulin, endorphins) Amino acid derivatives (e.g., epinephrine, norepinephrine)
text_html:
Chemical Nature of Hormones
Hormones have varied chemical structures:
  • Steroids (e.g., estrogens, androgens)
  • Polypeptides (e.g., insulin, endorphins)
  • Amino acid derivatives (e.g., epinephrine, norepinephrine)

subject: Chemistry
text: Insulin Function Insulin helps maintain blood glucose levels by responding to increases in glucose and keeping the levels within a narrow range.
text_html:
Insulin Function
Insulin helps maintain blood glucose levels by responding to increases in glucose and keeping the levels within a narrow range.

subject: Chemistry
text: Role of Glucagon Glucagon works with insulin to regulate blood glucose, increasing glucose when necessary.
text_html:
Role of Glucagon
Glucagon works with insulin to regulate blood glucose, increasing glucose when necessary.

subject: Chemistry
text: Hormonal Response to Stimuli Epinephrine and norepinephrine mediate responses to external stimuli.
text_html:
Hormonal Response to Stimuli
Epinephrine and norepinephrine mediate responses to external stimuli.

subject: Chemistry
text: Growth and Development Hormones Growth hormones and sex hormones are crucial for physical growth and development.
text_html:
Growth and Development Hormones
Growth hormones and sex hormones are crucial for physical growth and development.

subject: Chemistry
text: Thyroxine and Thyroid Health Produced by the thyroid gland, thyroxine levels (low and high) regulate metabolism, with iodine playing a key role in preventing thyroid disorders.
text_html:
Thyroxine and Thyroid Health
Produced by the thyroid gland, thyroxine levels (low and high) regulate metabolism, with iodine playing a key role in preventing thyroid disorders.

subject: Chemistry
text: Role of Steroid Hormones Steroid hormones from the adrenal cortex and gonads influence carbohydrate metabolism, inflammatory reactions, stress responses, and secondary sex characteristics.
text_html:
Role of Steroid Hormones
Steroid hormones from the adrenal cortex and gonads influence carbohydrate metabolism, inflammatory reactions, stress responses, and secondary sex characteristics.

subject: Chemistry
text: Addison's Disease Characterized by symptoms such as hypoglycemia and weakness, Addison's disease arises from adrenal cortex dysfunction and is treated with glucocorticoids and mineralocorticoids.
text_html:
Addison's Disease
Characterized by symptoms such as hypoglycemia and weakness, Addison's disease arises from adrenal cortex dysfunction and is treated with glucocorticoids and mineralocorticoids.

subject: Chemistry
text: Sex Hormones Produced by gonads, testosterone and estradiol govern secondary sexual characteristics, with progesterone preparing the uterus for pregnancy.
text_html:
Sex Hormones
Produced by gonads, testosterone and estradiol govern secondary sexual characteristics, with progesterone preparing the uterus for pregnancy.
